stories.headline: Typhoon Sinlaku Threatens Guam With Category 3-Equivalent Winds and Flooding
stories.summary:
Typhoon Sinlaku is tracking northwest through Micronesia and is expected to approach Guam early next week, with forecasters warning it could reach the equivalent of a Category 3 hurricane. AccuWeather International Meteorologist Tyler Roys described the storm as "a serious threat to the island of Guam," projecting destructive winds, heavy rainfall, power outages, and structural damage.

The U.S. Coast Guard has issued warnings for flooding and high winds, and has moved its cutters out of Apra Harbor to enable post-storm emergency response, including search and rescue operations. A typhoon watch warning of potential winds of 74 mph or more within 48 hours has been extended to nearby Rota, Tinian, and Saipan in the Northern Mariana Islands.

The Joint Typhoon Warning Center indicated Sinlaku could reach typhoon strength by Saturday and make its closest approach to Guam on Monday night into Tuesday. Officials in Guam said they were prepared to open emergency shelters as authorities urged residents to take precautions.

Flash flooding is considered a particular concern, as forecasters warn the storm could deliver heavy rain in a short period, with saturated ground also raising the risk of mudslides across the U.S. territory.

stories.headline: Israeli Strike Kills Infant Girl in South Lebanon During Father's Funeral
stories.summary:
An Israeli airstrike in south Lebanon killed an infant girl while mourners had gathered to bury her father, according to a Reuters report published Sunday. The strike also wounded other relatives at the funeral gathering, underscoring the continued danger in the border region despite broader ceasefire efforts.

Among the survivors was seven-year-old Aline Saeed, described as barely alive and wrapped in bloodied bandages, who had been present to attend her father's burial when the new strike hit. The attack highlights the fragility of any halt to hostilities along the Israel-Lebanon frontier.

The incident comes as Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has been reported to be pursuing direct negotiations with Lebanese officials, while cross-border clashes have continued. Critics of Israeli operations in Lebanon have cited civilian casualties as evidence of disproportionate force, while Israeli officials have maintained that strikes target Hezbollah infrastructure and personnel.

The reported killing of an infant at a funeral is likely to intensify international pressure on all parties to the Lebanon conflict, and may complicate U.S.-brokered efforts to stabilize the broader Middle East situation following the Iran ceasefire.

stories.headline: Indian Singing Legend Asha Bhosle Dies at 92
stories.summary:
Asha Bhosle, one of the most celebrated and prolific vocalists in the history of Indian cinema, has died at the age of 92, according to reports emerging Sunday. Her career spanned more than seven decades and encompassed an extraordinary range of genres, from classical and folk to film and pop music.

Bhosle was renowned as a playback singer for Bollywood films, recording tens of thousands of songs over her lifetime and earning recognition in the Guinness World Records for the most studio recordings by any artist. She was the younger sister of the equally legendary singer Lata Mangeshkar, who died in 2022.

Bhosle's death marks the passing of a towering figure in South Asian cultural history, whose voice defined generations of Indian popular music. Tributes were beginning to pour in from across India and from the global South Asian diaspora following the announcement.

No immediate cause of death was disclosed in early reports. Bhosle had remained a public figure well into her later years, continuing to perform and give interviews.

stories.headline: IMF and World Bank Set to Downgrade Global Growth Forecasts Amid Iran War Fallout
stories.summary:
Officials from the International Monetary Fund and World Bank have signaled they will downgrade their forecasts for global economic growth and raise inflation predictions, according to reports circulating ahead of a major gathering of economic policymakers in Washington. The revisions are linked in part to disruptions stemming from the U.S.-Iran conflict, including surging fuel and fertilizer costs.

The meetings come as U.S. consumer inflation ran hot in March, driven by a record surge in gasoline prices. Agricultural sectors in multiple countries are grappling with higher input costs, raising concerns about food price inflation in the months ahead, while Airports Council International in Europe warned of increasing concerns about jet fuel availability.

The Iran conflict has rippled through global energy markets, with Russia identified as a potential beneficiary due to higher energy prices and its access to cheap domestic fertilizer and diesel supplies. Ukraine's agricultural sector has been cited as particularly exposed, as farmers face surging import costs for materials they cannot source domestically.

The annual IMF and World Bank spring meetings in Washington are expected to be dominated by discussions of the economic damage caused by the Middle East conflict, compounding pre-existing concerns about trade policy disruptions and uneven global recovery.

stories.headline: U.S. Threatens to Block Strait of Hormuz After Iran Talks Collapse Without Nuclear Agreement
stories.summary:
The United States is threatening to block shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and has warned it is prepared to 'end' Iran after historic face-to-face negotiations in Islamabad, Pakistan, concluded Sunday without a deal. Vice President JD Vance, who led the U.S. delegation alongside special envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, said Tehran refused to commit to abandoning its nuclear weapons development program after more than 21 hours of talks.

Vance departed Islamabad Sunday morning, boarding Air Force Two after delivering a brief statement in which he said he had been in communication with President Trump and senior cabinet officials throughout the negotiations. The talks, brokered by Pakistan and held with Iranian Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf leading Tehran's delegation, represented the first direct U.S.-Iran engagement since the war began in late February.

The collapse leaves the fragile two-week ceasefire, announced April 7, in an increasingly precarious state. The United States had entered the talks demanding Iran renounce nuclear weapons development as a condition for any lasting peace agreement, while Iran had set its own preconditions before substantive negotiations could begin.

The threat to close the Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly a fifth of global oil supplies pass — raises the prospect of severe economic consequences for energy markets worldwide. The situation remains fluid, with no new round of talks announced and the ceasefire's status uncertain.

stories.headline: Trump Says U.S. 'Wins Regardless' as Iran Talks Fail, Heads to Florida
stories.summary:
President Trump, traveling to Florida on Sunday, commented publicly for the first time on the collapse of the Islamabad negotiations, telling reporters that the outcome of talks with Iran was of little concern to him. 'We win, regardless,' Trump said, signaling a posture of strategic indifference to whether a diplomatic resolution is reached.

The remarks come as Vice President Vance concluded the U.S. delegation's 21-hour engagement in Pakistan, which ended without Iran agreeing to abandon nuclear weapons development. Trump had spoken with Vance roughly a dozen times during the talks, according to Vance's own account at a brief Islamabad press conference.

The president's comments reflect the administration's stated position that military and economic pressure on Iran remains viable should diplomacy fail. Critics have argued the administration's maximalist demands made a deal structurally difficult from the outset, while supporters contend that accepting anything less than a full nuclear commitment would be a strategic failure.

With the ceasefire now under strain and U.S. threats to blockade the Strait of Hormuz escalating tensions, the administration's next steps on Iran policy are being closely watched by allies and adversaries alike.

stories.headline: Hungary Goes to the Polls as Orbán Faces Strongest Electoral Challenge of His Career
stories.summary:
Hungarian voters cast ballots Sunday in what is being described as the most consequential election in the country in a generation, with Prime Minister Viktor Orbán's Fidesz party facing a serious challenge from Péter Magyar's center-right Tisza party. Most independent polls show Tisza leading by double digits, though many analysts caution the result could be considerably closer given Fidesz's organizational strength and rural base.

Magyar, a 45-year-old lawyer and former Fidesz insider, closed his campaign Saturday before thousands of supporters in Debrecen, Hungary's second-largest city, calling the vote a day of 'resurrection' for Hungary and pledging national reconciliation if elected. Orbán, concluding his campaign on Budapest's Castle Hill, warned of external dangers and told supporters that 'now is not the time to take risks.'

Orbán has held power for 16 consecutive years and has reshaped Hungary's political and judicial institutions during that time, drawing sustained criticism from the European Union over rule-of-law concerns while maintaining strong domestic support among rural and conservative voters. A Tisza victory would end that era and mark a significant realignment within Central European politics.

Results are expected Sunday evening. International observers and EU officials are closely monitoring the vote, which carries implications not only for Hungary's domestic governance but also for its relationships with Brussels, Washington, and Moscow.

stories.headline: Machete Attack at Grand Central Station Injures Three; Suspect Shot and Killed by Police
stories.summary:
A man wielding a machete attacked three people at the 42nd Street-Grand Central subway station in New York City on Saturday morning before being fatally shot by police officers. The suspect, identified by authorities as Anthony Griffin, 44, was pronounced dead at Bellevue Hospital after officers shot him twice when he advanced on them with the weapon extended.

The three victims — an 84-year-old man, a 65-year-old man, and a 70-year-old woman — sustained lacerations in what police described as random attacks. One victim suffered significant facial lacerations and an open skull fracture; none of the injuries were considered life-threatening, according to Police Commissioner Jessica Tisch.

Tisch said officers ordered Griffin to drop the weapon at least 20 times and attempted to de-escalate before opening fire. Griffin had three prior unsealed arrests and was reportedly claiming to be 'Lucifer' during the encounter. New York Governor Kathy Hochul praised officers for acting quickly to stop the threat.

The attack occurred at one of the busiest transit hubs in the United States during Saturday morning commuting hours. The incident reignited ongoing discussions about subway safety in New York City, where transit crime and the policing response to mentally ill individuals in public spaces remain politically contentious issues.

stories.headline: Hungary Holds Landmark Election as Orbán Faces Strongest Challenge to 16-Year Rule
stories.summary:
Hungarian voters went to the polls Sunday in what analysts describe as the most consequential election in the country's post-communist history. Prime Minister Viktor Orbán's Fidesz party faces a serious challenge from Péter Magyar's center-right Tisza party, which has led by double digits in most independent polls.

Orbán and Magyar held their final campaign rallies Saturday, drawing large crowds in Budapest and Debrecen respectively. Orbán closed his campaign on Budapest's Castle Hill, warning of external dangers and urging voters not to 'take risks' during what he called 'an age of danger,' citing the ongoing conflict in neighboring Ukraine. Magyar, a 45-year-old lawyer and former Fidesz insider, struck a confident tone before thousands in Debrecen, calling the vote a 'day of resurrection' and pledging national reconciliation.

If Tisza wins, it would end Orbán's grip on power that began in 2010, a tenure marked by sweeping changes to Hungary's judicial system, media landscape, and relationship with the European Union. Many observers caution that the final result could be closer than polls suggest, as Fidesz retains strong organizational infrastructure and deep rural support.

The outcome carries broad implications for European politics, NATO cohesion, and Hungary's relationship with the Trump administration, which has maintained warmer ties with Orbán than other EU leaders. Results are expected throughout Sunday evening.

stories.headline: OpenAI Pauses Major UK Data Centre Project Over Regulatory Climate and Energy Costs
stories.summary:
OpenAI has suspended plans for its primary data centre project in the United Kingdom, dealing a significant blow to the British government's ambitions to establish the country as a leading global hub for artificial intelligence. The decision was attributed to what the company described as an unfavourable regulatory environment and prohibitively high energy costs.

The move is a setback for Prime Minister Keir Starmer's government, which has made AI investment a centrepiece of its economic growth agenda and hosted an AI safety summit to attract major technology players. UK officials have not yet responded publicly to OpenAI's announcement.

The withdrawal underscores mounting tensions between major AI developers and regulators in Europe and the UK over data governance, energy infrastructure demands, and compliance costs. Data centres required for large-scale AI model training consume enormous amounts of electricity, and energy pricing in the UK is among the highest in the developed world.

OpenAI has not indicated whether the pause is permanent or whether it plans to renegotiate terms with UK authorities. The company continues to expand data infrastructure investments in the United States and other markets.

stories.headline: Japan Commits $16 Billion to Rapidus Semiconductor Push in Global AI Chip Race
stories.summary:
Japan's government has pledged approximately $16 billion to accelerate the development of Rapidus, the country's flagship domestic semiconductor manufacturer, as Tokyo moves aggressively to reduce reliance on foreign chip supply chains. The investment represents one of the largest state commitments to semiconductor manufacturing in Japanese history.

Rapidus, a consortium backed by major Japanese corporations, is aiming to produce cutting-edge two-nanometer chips domestically by the late 2020s. The company is pursuing a partnership with IBM for technology transfer and has been constructing a fabrication facility in Hokkaido.

The announcement comes amid intensifying global competition for AI chip dominance, with the United States, South Korea, Taiwan, and China all investing heavily in domestic semiconductor capacity. Japan's bet on Rapidus is widely seen as a strategic response both to supply disruptions exposed during the COVID-19 pandemic and to the growing geopolitical risks surrounding Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company.

Analysts have questioned whether Rapidus can close the technology gap with established leaders such as TSMC and Samsung within the stated timeline, but Japanese officials argue that sustained public investment is essential to securing national technology sovereignty.

stories.headline: UK Pauses Plan to Cede Chagos Islands to Mauritius Following U.S. Opposition
stories.summary:
The United Kingdom has suspended legislation that would transfer sovereignty of the Chagos Islands to Mauritius, according to The Times, after the deal encountered opposition from the Trump administration. The planned bill will not be included in the British government's next parliamentary agenda.

The Chagos Islands are home to Diego Garcia, one of the most strategically important joint U.S.-UK military bases in the world, used for long-range bomber operations and intelligence gathering across the Indian Ocean and Middle East. The Trump administration had previously signalled strong objections to the deal, arguing it could jeopardize American military operations.

The original agreement, negotiated under the previous Conservative government and carried forward by Labour, was framed as resolving a long-standing colonial dispute and securing a 99-year lease for the military base. Critics, including the Trump administration, warned that ceding sovereignty could give adversarial nations — particularly China — greater influence in the region.

The pause marks a significant diplomatic retreat for the Starmer government and raises questions about the future of the agreement with Mauritius, which has long claimed sovereignty over the archipelago under international law rulings.

stories.headline: Trump Administration Fires Immigration Judges in Latest Reshaping of U.S. Immigration Courts
stories.summary:
The Trump administration has abruptly dismissed a number of immigration judges, according to the New York Times and The Guardian, marking the latest in a series of moves to overhaul the nation's immigration court system. The dismissals are part of a broader effort by the administration to reshape adjudication of immigration cases in line with its enforcement priorities.

Immigration courts, which operate under the Justice Department's Executive Office for Immigration Review rather than the independent federal judiciary, have long faced a massive backlog of cases. Administration officials have argued that reforming the courts is necessary to accelerate removals and enforce immigration law more effectively.

Critics, including immigration attorneys and civil liberties groups, have condemned the firings as an assault on judicial independence and due process, warning that removing experienced judges will worsen backlogs and expose migrants to arbitrary outcomes. Supporters of the administration contend that the existing corps of immigration judges has impeded enforcement and that the courts require personnel aligned with current policy.

The dismissals come as the administration pursues aggressive deportation operations nationwide and continues to contest court orders in multiple jurisdictions that have challenged aspects of its immigration enforcement program.
